Per the dispatch brief's evidence threshold, I will infer all stakeholder needs directly from the dispatch context provided. No external research is required or permitted.

**Preamble**: Performing stakeholder need analysis for platform engineers as portal users and maintainers, within the explicit/implicit-only scope.

---

## Stakeholder Need Analysis: Internal Developer Portal

### Dispatch Validation

| Checklist Item | Status | Notes |
|----------------|--------|-------|
| Objective is one sentence | ✓ | Analyze platform engineer stakeholder needs for developer portal strategic scoping |
| Stakeholder scope is explicit | ✓ | 14 platform engineers across 3 sub-groups; dual role as users and maintainers |
| Need layer is stated | ✓ | Explicit and implicit only; latent/constraints out of scope |
| Slice boundary is explicit | ✓ | Platform engineers only; product team needs excluded |
| Why it matters is stated | ✓ | Feeds scoper_lead's strategic brief for go/no-go decision |
| Evidence threshold is stated | ✓ | Infer from dispatch context; no external research |
| Output schema is stated | ✓ | Stakeholder map, layered needs, fit criteria, non-goals |
| Stop condition is stated | ✓ | All needs have layer classification and fit criteria |
| Chaining budget is stated | ✓ | None granted; complete in own context |

**Stop condition confirmed**: Proceeding to analysis.

---

## 1. Stakeholder Map

### Primary Stakeholder: Platform Engineers

**Role**: Internal infrastructure and tooling caretakers who both consume and maintain the developer portal.

**Composition** (from dispatch context):
- 14 engineers total across three sub-groups
- **Infrastructure** (CI/CD pipelines, cloud)
- **Developer Experience** (package registries, local tooling)
- **Reliability** (observability, incident response)

**Context**:
- Serve as internal customers to six product teams (~200 engineers total)
- Maintain shared systems that product teams depend on daily
- Currently absorbing 30–40% of their time on repetitive knowledge transfer
- Organization grew from 50 to 200 engineers in 18 months, breaking informal knowledge-sharing mechanisms

**Incentives as Portal Users** (consumer role):
- Reduce time spent answering repetitive questions from product teams
- Enable product teams to self-serve tooling discovery without escalating to a platform engineer
- Find answers faster when they themselves need to use shared infrastructure

**Incentives as Portal Maintainers** (author/curator role):
- Ensure portal content remains accurate and current without requiring dedicated staffing
- Minimize ongoing portal maintenance overhead
- Avoid the portal becoming another stale documentation graveyard (a known failure mode)
- Maintain authority over their own system documentation without editorial bottleneck

**Conflict to Surface**: The dual role creates tension — platform engineers want the portal to reduce their burden, but also fear that maintaining the portal will become additional unpaid labor. This conflict is structural, not personal.

---

## 2. Layered Needs

### 2a. Explicit Needs

All explicit needs are traced to specific statements in the dispatch context.

---

**Explicit Need 1**: Reduce time consumed by repetitive product-team questions

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Layer** | Explicit |
| **Source** | "Platform engineers report spending 30-40% of their time answering the same questions from product teams" |
| **Confidence** | High — directly stated by stakeholder population |
| **Job-to-be-done** | "When a product team asks me a question I've answered ten times before, I want to point them to documentation rather than retype the answer, so I can spend that time on platform work." |
| **Fit Criterion** | A platform engineer can redirect a recurring product-team question to a portal page and observe that the same question does not recur within the same sprint (observable: question volume decrease; testable: track question ticket count per topic before/after portal launch) |

---

**Explicit Need 2**: Self-serve tooling discovery for product teams

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Layer** | Explicit |
| **Source** | "informal knowledge-sharing mechanisms that worked at smaller scale (Slack channels, tribal knowledge, ad-hoc documentation) are breaking down" |
| **Confidence** | High — failure of informal mechanisms is directly stated |
| **Job-to-be-done** | "When a product team engineer needs to configure CI/CD, set up a package registry, or understand service mesh options, they want to discover what tooling exists and how to use it without asking a platform engineer." |
| **Fit Criterion** | A product team engineer can find documentation for a shared platform capability (e.g., "how do I set up CI/CD for service X") by searching the portal without escalating to a platform engineer (observable: search-to-answer ratio; testable: portal search analytics + reduced escalation tickets) |

---

**Explicit Need 3**: Centralized, durable documentation for shared infrastructure

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Layer** | Explicit |
| **Source** | "ad-hoc documentation" breaking down; "platform engineers report spending 30-40% of their time answering the same questions" |
| **Confidence** | High — documentation gap is implicit in the problem statement |
| **Job-to-be-done** | "When I need to document a new shared tool or update an existing configuration, I want a single authoritative place to publish it where product teams will actually look." |
| **Fit Criterion** | Each of the three platform sub-groups (infrastructure, developer experience, reliability) can publish documentation for their systems in the portal and verify it appears in the appropriate section within one publish action (observable: doc publish workflow completion; testable: content appears in correct section post-publish) |

---

### 2b. Implicit Needs

Implicit needs surface assumptions stakeholders hold but have not articulated. Each is inferred from structural conditions in the dispatch context.

---

**Implicit Need 1**: Portal maintainability without dedicated staffing

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Layer** | Implicit |
| **Inference basis** | Portal is maintained by platform engineers who already report 30-40% time spent on knowledge transfer; no additional headcount is mentioned; if portal maintenance requires significant ongoing effort, it adds burden rather than reducing it |
| **Assumption held** | The portal can be kept current through the natural workflow of platform engineers without requiring a designated documentation owner or dedicated staffing |
| **Confidence** | Medium — inferred from the constraint that no additional staffing is mentioned; alternative interpretation is that dedicated ops would be acceptable |
| **Fit Criterion** | A platform engineer can update a documentation page for their system in under 15 minutes as part of their normal workflow (observable: doc update time; testable: time-motion measurement of typical doc update) without triggering a separate review or approval pipeline |

---

**Implicit Need 2**: Integration with existing tools rather than replacement

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Layer** | Implicit |
| **Inference basis** | Platform engineers maintain CI/CD pipelines, package registries, service mesh, observability tooling — these are existing systems with established workflows; portal adoption requires fitting into engineers' existing habits |
| **Assumption held** | The portal will integrate with or reference existing tools (linking to Jenkins configs, Nexus repositories, Datadog dashboards) rather than abstracting them into a new monolithic interface |
| **Confidence** | High — any portal that requires engineers to abandon existing toolchains would face adoption resistance |
| **Fit Criterion** | A portal page for any shared system links directly to the system's existing management interface (e.g., Jenkins job config, Nexus repo URL) rather than duplicating its state (observable: external link presence on portal pages; testable: audit of portal pages for live links to source systems) |

---

**Implicit Need 3**: Adoption incentives that motivate product teams to use the portal

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Layer** | Implicit |
| **Inference basis** | Platform engineers are frustrated by product teams asking questions rather than self-serving; this implies product teams currently prefer asking directly; for the portal to reduce platform engineer burden, product teams must actually use it |
| **Assumption held** | Product teams will choose to use the portal over asking a platform engineer directly if the portal provides sufficient value or if asking directly becomes more costly/awkward |
| **Confidence** | Medium — adoption mechanics are not described; portal could succeed through pull (value) or push (policy) mechanisms |
| **Fit Criterion** | Within 30 days of portal launch, at least 60% of product team engineers have performed at least one self-serve action (search, lookup, or walkthrough completion) per week (observable: portal active user ratio; testable: weekly unique user analytics from product team cohort) |

---

**Implicit Need 4**: Content governance that prevents the portal from becoming stale

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Layer** | Implicit |
| **Inference basis** | The dispatch context explicitly identifies "ad-hoc documentation" as failing; the fear of "another stale documentation graveyard" is a known failure mode in internal tooling; platform engineers as maintainers implicitly fear their content will rot |
| **Assumption held** | Stale content is worse than no content because it trains users to ignore the portal; the portal must have lightweight mechanisms to signal when content is outdated |
| **Confidence** | High — this is a well-documented failure mode for internal developer portals |
| **Fit Criterion** | When a platform engineer marks a documentation page as "applies to: [version/service X]", the portal displays a freshness indicator (last verified date or version tag) visible to readers (observable: freshness indicator presence; testable: visual audit of content display) |

---

## 3. Fit Criteria Summary

All fit criteria are observable, testable, and framed in stakeholder terms (what a stakeholder can verify) rather than system terms (what the system must do).

| ID | Fit Criterion | Testable Via |
|----|---------------|--------------|
| FC-1 | A platform engineer can redirect a recurring product-team question to a portal page and observe that the same question does not recur within the same sprint | Question ticket count tracking per topic |
| FC-2 | A product team engineer can find documentation for a shared platform capability by searching the portal without escalating to a platform engineer | Portal search analytics + escalation ticket reduction |
| FC-3 | Each platform sub-group can publish documentation for their systems and verify it appears in the appropriate section within one publish action | Content publish workflow audit |
| FC-4 | A platform engineer can update a documentation page in under 15 minutes as part of their normal workflow | Time-motion measurement of doc updates |
| FC-5 | A portal page for any shared system links directly to the system's existing management interface | External link presence audit |
| FC-6 | Within 30 days of portal launch, at least 60% of product team engineers perform at least one self-serve action per week | Weekly active user analytics |
| FC-7 | When content is version-tagged, the portal displays a freshness indicator visible to readers | Visual audit of content display |

---

## 4. Non-Goals

Non-goals are explicit boundaries that prevent downstream scope drift. Each includes justification for why exclusion is load-bearing.

### Non-Goal 1: Replacing existing CI/CD, package registry, service mesh, or observability tooling

**Justification**: The portal surfaces existing infrastructure and tooling. The dispatch context specifies platform engineers maintain these systems; the portal's value is in discovery and documentation, not in becoming another instance of the tooling itself. If the portal were to replace Jenkins, Nexus, or Istio, it would become the very burden it was meant to reduce. This non-goal is load-bearing because without it, the portal scope expands from "documentation and discovery" to "infrastructure reconstruction."

---

### Non-Goal 2: Building a social platform, communication tool, or real-time chat system

**Justification**: The problem is repetitive questions consuming platform engineer time. The solution space is structured documentation and self-serve discovery. A social or communication layer (forums, chat integration, "ask an expert" features) would add maintenance surface area for the same 14 engineers and risks recreating the Slack-channel fragmentation the portal is meant to cure. This non-goal is load-bearing because social features have high engagement drag and low durability as documentation.

---

### Non-Goal 3: Redesigning the organizational structure of platform engineering or product teams

**Justification**: The dispatch context identifies scaling as the root cause of informal mechanism breakdown. The portal is a tooling response to organizational scale; it does not require restructuring who reports to whom or how sub-groups are organized. This non-goal is load-bearing because organizational redesign is outside the scoper_lead's mandate and would stall the initiative indefinitely if treated as in-scope.

---

### Non-Goal 4: Addressing product team needs directly (feature requests, product team-facing UX, product team workflows)

**Justification**: The dispatch brief explicitly limits this slice to platform engineer stakeholder needs. Product teams are the beneficiaries of the portal, not its maintainers or primary users in this phase. Expanding to product-team-facing features (product roadmaps, team-specific dashboards, customer-facing integration docs) would dilute the portal's focus on reducing platform engineer burden. This non-goal is load-bearing because it defines the slice boundary of this analysis — a follow-up dispatch is explicitly reserved for latent needs, and a separate stakeholder analysis for product teams is the appropriate path for their needs.

---

### Non-Goal 5: Proposing a specific technology stack, vendor, or architectural pattern

**Justification**: The dispatch brief prohibits solution proposals. Fit criteria are stakeholder-framed and technology-agnostic. Specifying a vendor (e.g., Backstage vs. custom) or architectural choice (microservices vs. monolith) would pre-empt the architecture review that is gated on this analysis. This non-goal is load-bearing because it prevents the needs analysis from collapsing into a solution preference.

---

## 5. Conflicts and Gaps

### Conflict: Dual-role incentive tension
Platform engineers want the portal to reduce their burden, but as maintainers they fear it will become additional unpaid labor. This is a structural conflict that must be visible to the scoper_lead — it cannot be resolved by defining requirements, only by ensuring the portal's maintenance model is genuinely lightweight.

### Gap: Adoption mechanics are underspecified
The dispatch context does not describe how product teams will be motivated to use the portal. Fit criterion FC-6 sets a 60% adoption threshold but does not specify whether this is achieved through pull (portal value) or push (policy mandate). This gap affects architecture significantly and should be flagged.

### Gap: Content governance mechanism is unspecified
The implicit need for freshness indicators (FC-7) is identified, but who has authority to mark content stale, and what happens when content is stale, is not defined. This is a known risk: portals die when governance is unclear.

---

## 6. Self-Validation Log

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| Every need has layer classification (explicit or implicit) | ✓ All 7 needs classified |
| Every need has evidence traceable to dispatch context | ✓ All explicit needs cite specific statements; implicit needs cite structural inference basis |
| Every fit criterion is observable and testable | ✓ All 7 fit criteria include testable verification method |
| Every non-goal has justification | ✓ All 5 non-goals include load-bearing rationale |
| No solution proposals | ✓ Verified: zero technology recommendations, zero implementation suggestions |
| No scope expansion to product teams | ✓ Confirmed: product team needs explicitly excluded |
| Stakeholder dual role is explicitly modeled | ✓ User role and maintainer role separated with distinct incentive sets |
| Confidence levels are stated | ✓ All needs include confidence (High/Medium) |

---

## Stop Condition Met

All identified needs (7 total: 3 explicit, 4 implicit) have been classified and have corresponding fit criteria. The analysis is complete and ready for return to the scoper_lead.","time":{"start":1775686274286,"end":1775686274286}}}
